retrieved_docs.title: RB-007 — API Latency p99 Degradation
retrieved_docs.excerpt:
# RB-007 — API Latency p99 Degradation

**Service:** API / Backend  
**Severity:** High  
**Tags:** latency, performance, p99, slow-requests

## Symptoms
- p99 latency alert fires (e.g., `> 2s` for endpoints that normally run `< 200ms`)
- p50 latency unchanged — only tail latency affected
- Distributed trace shows a specific downstream call as the bottleneck
- Users report intermittent slowness or timeouts

## Possible Causes
- Slow downstream dependency (DB, LLM API, vector store) affecting tail requests
- Lock contention in database (long-running queries blocking others)
- GC pressure in JVM-based service
- Large payload processing (embedding generation, log parsing) blocking async workers
- Missing database index causing occasional full table scans

## Diagnostic Steps

```bash
# Identify which endpoint has the highest p99
# Prometheus query:
histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))

# Break down by endpoint path to find the culprit
histogram_quantile(0.99, sum by (path) (rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])))
```

```bash
# Check distributed traces (Langfuse or OpenTelemetry)
# Look for spans with duration > 1s in the last 30 minutes

#

retrieved_docs.title: PM-170 — incident.io
retrieved_docs.excerpt:
# PM-170 — incident.io

**Company:** incident.io  
**Category:** Uncategorized  
**Source:** https://incident.io/blog/clouds-caches-and-connection-conundrums

## Incident Summary

After moving to Google Cloud they saw spikes of Postgres connection timeouts (~200 new connections/s) and memcached "i/o timeout" errors. Pool tuning (15m→30m max lifetime, static pool size, `MaxIdleConns` 2→20) helped each in turn but didn't eliminate the cache errors. The smoking gun was GKE Dataplane V2: bursts of parallel outbound calls (made worse by an accidental N+1 join) saturated the per-node `anetd` agent's CPU, dropping packets between the node and other services running on it.

retrieved_docs.title: PM-191 — OpenAI
retrieved_docs.excerpt:
# PM-191 — OpenAI

**Company:** OpenAI  
**Category:** Uncategorized  
**Source:** https://web.archive.org/web/20240426015133/https://openai.com/blog/march-20-chatgpt-outage

## Incident Summary

Queues for requests and responses in a Redis cache became corrupted and out of sequence, leading to some requests revealing other people's user data to some users, including app activity data and some billing info.

retrieved_docs.title: PM-083 — CircleCI
retrieved_docs.excerpt:
# PM-083 — CircleCI

**Company:** CircleCI  
**Category:** Database  
**Source:** https://discuss.circleci.com/t/post-incident-report-april-4-2025-delays-in-starting-workflows/53113

## Incident Summary

A blue/green upgrade of the workflows database succeeded mechanically, but the post-cutover database was running every query against disk because its statistics tables had not been updated. The team ran `ANALYZE` early in the upgrade procedure, but a second major-version upgrade in the same deployment then made those statistics stale, leaving the planner without usable indexes after the cutover. Workflows latency spiked, jobs were dropped after exhausting their 10-minute retry, and the team eventually re-promoted the old (blue) database to recover.

retrieved_docs.title: PM-192 — PagerDuty
retrieved_docs.excerpt:
# PM-192 — PagerDuty

**Company:** PagerDuty  
**Category:** Uncategorized  
**Source:** https://web.archive.org/web/20211019062735/https://www.pagerduty.com/blog/outage-post-mortem-april-13-2013/

## Incident Summary

In April 2013, PagerDuty, a cloud service proving application uptime monitoring and real-time notifications, suffered an outage when two of its three independent cloud deployments in different data centers began experiencing connectivity issues and high network latency. It was found later that the two independent deployments shared a common peering point which was experiencing network instability. While the third deployment was still operational, PagerDuty's applications failed to establish quorum due to to high network latency and hence failed in their ability to send notifications.

retrieved_docs.title: PM-167 — Honeycomb
retrieved_docs.excerpt:
# PM-167 — Honeycomb

**Company:** Honeycomb  
**Category:** Uncategorized  
**Source:** https://www.honeycomb.io/blog/incident-review-shepherd-cache-delays/

## Incident Summary

On September 8th, 2022, our ingest system went down repeatedly and caused interruptions for over eight hours. We will first cover the background behind the incident with a high-level view of the relevant architecture, how we tried to investigate and fix the system, and finally, we’ll go over some meaningful elements that surfaced from our incident review process.

root_causes.description: Replication lag on postgres-replica-2 caused read-after-write consistency violation. Users' profile updates were successfully committed to postgres-primary, but reads from postgres-replica-2 returned stale data due to 45-second replication lag, making it appear that updates did not persist.
evidence: Replication lag on postgres-replica-2 measured at 45 seconds (baseline <1s) at 12:02:00, a 45x increase from normal
Profile-api reads for this endpoint are routed to postgres-replica-2 for load balancing, while writes go to postgres-primary
Writes confirmed committed on postgres-primary at 12:03:00 with normal write throughput and latency
User reports of 'profile update didn't save' despite UI confirming success indicate successful write acknowledgment but failed read verification

root_causes.description: Heavy I/O contention from the nightly ETL export job running against postgres-replica-2 since 11:50 caused replication lag by starving the replication process of I/O resources, preventing timely application of write-ahead logs from the primary.
evidence: Large nightly export job running against postgres-replica-2 since 11:50 consuming heavy I/O at 12:04:00
Replication lag spike to 45 seconds occurred during the same time window as the heavy ETL job execution
Baseline replication lag is <1s, indicating the replica normally keeps up; the spike correlates with the resource-intensive export job
PM-083 (CircleCI) demonstrates how resource contention and query planning issues can cause database performance degradation and latency spikes

root_causes.description: Lack of read-after-write consistency enforcement in the profile-api service architecture. The service does not implement mechanisms (e.g., read-your-writes consistency, sticky reads to primary, or consistency tokens) to ensure users read their own writes, leaving the application vulnerable to replication lag.
evidence: Profile-api routes reads to postgres-replica-2 for load balancing without apparent read-after-write consistency guarantees
The incident manifests as a consistency violation: writes succeed on primary but reads from replica return stale data
No evidence of circuit breakers, fallback to primary reads, or consistency tokens when replication lag exceeds threshold
RB-007 identifies slow downstream dependencies as a common cause of latency issues; this case shows how replica lag becomes a consistency issue rather than just latency

contributing_factors: Nightly ETL export job scheduled during peak user activity hours (11:50 PM start, but incident reported at 12:00 AM) without resource isolation or I/O throttling
Lack of monitoring/alerting on replication lag threshold that would trigger automatic failover or read routing changes before user impact
No read-after-write consistency SLA or timeout mechanism to detect and mitigate replica lag in real-time
Single replica (postgres-replica-2) used for load balancing without redundancy; if this replica lags, all read traffic is affected
Absence of circuit breaker or fallback logic to route reads back to postgres-primary when replica lag exceeds acceptable threshold (e.g., >5 seconds)

retrieved_docs.title: PM-151 — Gitlab
retrieved_docs.excerpt:
# PM-151 — Gitlab

**Company:** Gitlab  
**Category:** Uncategorized  
**Source:** https://about.gitlab.com/2017/02/10/postmortem-of-database-outage-of-january-31/

## Incident Summary

Influx of requests overloaded the database, caused replication to lag, tired admin deleted the wrong directory, six hours of data lost. See also [earlier report](https://about.gitlab.com/2017/02/01/gitlab-dot-com-database-incident) and [HN discussion](https://web.archive.org/web/20240918153505/https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=13537052).
